data.title: Tesla's First Annual Revenue Drop Comes Amidst Massive Changes
data.description:
On the 28th of January 2026, EV carmaker and Magnificent Seven constituent Tesla Inc. (TSLA) released its Q4 2025 earnings release, which had a first for the company: its first FY revenue decline in year-on-year (YoY) terms.

Trend Drilldown

2025 saw the repetition of 2024, in that revenues from sales and leasing of Tesla vehicles registered lower than the previous year’s.

Meanwhile “Services and Other” – constituting maintenance services, part sales and Supercharging network uses – continued to rise, as did Energy, which constitutes the company’s energy storage products.

The drop in sales is met by a nearly similar drop in the cost of revenue. However, what tipped the bottom line was a substantial increase in operating expenses, which was mostly led by increasing spending on Research & Development (R&D). As a result, for the second year in a row now, net income attributable to shareholders is down by half of that from the previous year.

The company’s ongoing reliance on automotive sales remains a source of non-diversified risk: despite advances made by both “Services” and “Energy”, large swings in automotive sales remain the clearest barometer of bottom-line benefit to shareholders.

Overall production and deliveries in 2025 fell by 7% and 9%, respectively, from the volumes achieved in 2024, which themselves were 4% and 1% lower than in 2023.

While the company states that they achieved record deliveries in the Asia-Pacific (APAC) region, Europe is likely a significant factor in the loss of sales: Chinese automaker BYD has made massive strides in penetration while Volkswagen continues to consolidate sales in the continent.

Nearly 1 in 10 vehicles produced by Volkswagen globally are Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and around 76% of VW’s BEV sales were in Europe, a market that continues to show resilient growth in sales.

While petrol- and diesel-powered vehicle sales have been running nearly flat for two years, BEV sales in Europe have returned to double-digit growth percentages in 2025.

Interestingly, the company’s free cash flow—calculated as the difference in net cash from operating activities and the net cash used in investing activities—has now seen a massive surge to $6.2 billion, which essentially recoups the previous two full years of negative flows. On top of that, the company has ambitious plans for 2026.

What’s Ahead for Tesla

Tesla indicated that it intends to achieve new milestones in its transformation from an “EV manufacturer” to a massive "AI and Energy" company. In the first half of 2026, it plans to more than double the size of onsite computation capacity in Texas by building out Cortex 2, a massive AI computation cluster for training its Optimus humanoid robots as well as providing an end-to-end neural network for its “Cybercabs,” purpose-built, fully autonomous two-seaters designed specifically for Tesla’s "Robotaxi" service. While Tesla intends to operate the majority of these vehicles, individual owners will also be allowed to join the network. Tesla has already logged over 650,000 miles in its pilot robotaxi service in Austin, Texas.

The company also stated that it intends to start production of the Optimus, a general-purpose bipedal humanoid robot, before the end of 2026. Conceived as an augment for human manual labour, the company states that its mass production-ready “Gen 3” will be unveiled in Q1 2026. The eventual production capacity is planned to be 1 million robots per year.

Managing the robots and Cybertaxis in the future will be the AI5 and AI6 inference chips developed in-house, with production planned for 2027 and 2028, respectively, which also potentially limits its client relationship with Nvidia in the future.

Over on the battery pack front, the company stated that it has begun to produce the “4680” cells and electrodes needed to manufacture battery packs. It expects to begin production of Lithium Iron phosphate [LFP] batteries as well as the cathodes for said battery packs in U.S.-based facilities in 2026.

If the relative staleness of Tesla’s catalogue was potentially a factor in the downturn of sales over the past two years, the company assures that there might be cause for cheer: “production ”ramps”—meaning a move from prototypes to scaling production lines for high-volume output—of the Tesla Semi and Cybertruck are both commencing in the first half of 2026, as is the production of the next-generation Roadster.

In Conclusion

While the continuing fall of BEV sales is certainly a cause for concern among investors seeking a foothold in the EV boom, the lineup being announced makes the value proposition for Tesla as "just" an EV manufacturer a lot more complicated: both Semi and Cybercab are commercial products more suited for fleet operators than individual owners. On the one hand, commercial buyers are more resilient than individuals. On the other, it requires a lot more deftness in managing relations, service, and more.

The stated rollout of Cybercabs under Tesla's banner, i.e., as an owner-operator of fleet services, is an interesting development. But it remains uncertain as to exactly how extensive this fleet can be; most jurisdictions in the United States (or anywhere else, for that matter) have been resistant to the risks inherent with fully-autonomous driving.

The stated plan to begin producing robots for consumption (presumably first in industrial settings) is yet another evolution that is quite far removed from its value proposition as a carmaker. Whether these robots can truly augment manual labour on a factory floor remains to be seen. So far, Tesla has only stated using these robots on their U.S. factory floor.

It might be entirely in order to stop considering Tesla as just a carmaker and think of it as an enterprise exploring complex high-tech endeavours in general. But that might mean the stock goes from being a "value" ticker to a more speculative ticker that is essentially promising growth with no history to back said narrative.

All in all, the stock could face substantial volatility in the months or quarters to come. A "Hold" might be in order.

data.title: Gilat Satellite Networks: Upside Case Built On IFC, Not Momentum
data.description:
Gilat Satellite Networks (GILT), a small satellite broadband solutions provider from Israel, is up ~200% this past year. This may make investors think that its upside potential is already over. This stance has merit - after such a run, GILT no longer looks cheap. However, there is a reason this could be wrong. Despite its spike, it could well be that the market is misclassifying GILT as just another cyclical satellite ground-equipment vendor, whereas its core growth - in years to come - might well be in IFC or in-flight connectivity.

Q3 results seem to indicate that something may be amiss with the received view. Yes, revenue did grow 58% year-over-year, guidance got raised, and both backlog and liquidity were strengthened. However, reported margins remained narrow, and there’s a disconnect between earnings and earnings potential, at least in the mid-term. These things indicate that Gilat is in the middle - not the end - of a transition.

If that be so, the current run only reflects the market’s faith in execution potential, not complete implementation of it. The market still doesn’t discount the company’s IFC-led growth, its multi-orbit platform, and a lineup of large orders from aviation to military to communication.

Not a SATCOM vendor any more

It is interesting to note from GILT’s Q3 results that much of the 116% YoY growth in the commercial segment has come from in-flight connectivity (IFC). While management did not break out the in line numbers, they were unusually explicit that the Q3 growth was “primarily driven by the in-flight connectivity vertical.” It can be understood that a bulk of this growth came from the $60+ million Sidewinder ESA orders, the $7+ million IFC system orders, and rapid aircraft deployment growth with 350+ aircraft, 300k+ flight hours.

This is a major shift in Gilat’s business, from the cyclical, one-time nature of SATCOM sales to the longer-term IFC business. This shift did not emerge suddenly in Q3. As early as Q1, management was already identifying in-flight connectivity as one of Gilat’s main growth engines, alongside defense and NGSO constellations - a notable departure from its historical positioning as a generic satellite ground-equipment vendor. In Q1, management said that they were already seeing “...the benefits of this change, particularly in our main growth engines, defense, VHTS and NGSO constellations and in-flight connectivity.” In Q2, they said they were seeing “increasing demand for IFC solutions and the widespread adoption of GEO, MEO, and LEO architectures.”

IFC is not merely a one-time sale of hardware. Aircrafts have long lifecycles. There are upgrades needed, new machines are added to fleets, and modern content needs extra bandwidth. This creates recurring business.

The very large deployment of Gilat’s Stellar Blu Sidewinder ESA terminals makes it a commercial platform, not a pilot project. Revenue is now durable and with better margins. The shift from retrofit to line-fit adds to that margin.

The valuation, despite the stock’s huge rise last year, still does not take this growth potential into account. It is still based on improved SATCOM execution - which is a fact. But it is not taking into account IFC expansion.

Stellar Blu: Current Margin Dip a Feature, Not a Bug

If an investor wants to dismiss Gilat’s bull case as “already priced in” to the stock, they have to explain one thing - why did margins fall when growth surged? In Q3, Gilat’s GAAP margin fell 7%, from 37% a year earlier, and non-GAAP margin also fell 6% (from 38% a year earlier). This was despite the 116% growth I mentioned before. Management explains this as due to lower margins at Stellar Blu. Lower margins came due to ramp up inefficiencies. Some amortization expenses were also there.

This margin pressure did not happen suddenly. It can be traced quarter by quarter. So in Q1, GAAP gross margin fell 6%, and management again explained this as due to Stellar Blu ramp + acquisition amortization + lower Peru margin. Again in Q2, non-GAAP margin fell 4%, and management had the same explanation.

There is consistency in management’s explanation of margin pressure as a ramp-up issue, not any weakness in demand. This should be looked at through the EBITDA frame, something management themselves is doing. In Q1, adjusted EBITDA was $7.6 million, but if you exclude Stellar Blu, it came to $11.2 million. That means, Stellar Blu accounted for $3.6 million in losses. In other words, Gilat’s core business improved year over year, while the newly acquired IFC unit was temporarily dilutive during its production ramp.

The same thing was repeated with slightly different numbers in Q2. In Q3, management did not clearly disclose those numbers, but they did say that Stellar Blu losses were narrowing. They also guided to profitability beginning in Q4. This is standard management language once a ramp moves closer to breakeven.

This takes us back to what I had been saying earlier - core profitability improved, driving higher multiples, but the new IFC segment is still scaling, and valuation hasn’t adjusted for that.

My valuation disconnect thesis is also strengthened by figuring out why Stellar Blu is dilutive right now. The reason is simple - it is due to the ramping up of production, not due to lower demand. This ramp up is creating certain operational pressures - mostly to do with component qualification and early-unit costs, with output running below long-term targets. Efficiencies of scale have still not set in, even though demand for higher scale already exists. On top of that, IFC revenue in 2025 was still retrofit-based. Line-fit programs - which I noted are higher margin, stickier, etc - will start to scale from 2026. So we are in an interesting and valuable situation - there is a backlog, yet there is margin pressure. That only means, in time, demand will drive up margins at scale - and valuation will reflect that.

Multi-Orbit is already generating revenue

While growth is coming from IFC, its durability comes from Gilat’s multi-orbit capability, which is already generating contracted revenue from multiple end markets.

Thus, in 2025, Gilat received over $40 million in orders from a leading satellite operator for its SkyEdge IV platform. This spanned multi-orbit deployments of GEO, MEO and LEO constellations, as well as fresh, new SkyEdge platform orders in Asia-Pacific. These were not replacement GEO orders, but orders for next-gen ground networks. Gilat also received support orders for LEO - all of which indicates how its multi-orbit is being purchased as a core architectural requirement by major customers. Besides satellite operators, Multi Orbit's durable revenue is also validated by durable, broad-based aviation sales.

This flexibility has critical implications for earnings. Because these are broad-scoped, multi-orbit revenues, they are stickier. That’s because Gilat embeds deeper into customer networks, and replacement is difficult.

Defence weakness is temporary, not structural

Defence revenue declined YoY, even when total company revenue grew 59%. However, defence-related demand did not collapse - this was just a timing gap between contract awards and revenue recognition - a common occurrence in defence businesses.

Case in point: Gilat received over $7 million in orders to supply transportable SATCOM terminals to the U.S. Army, followed by additional defense orders exceeding $7 million for U.S. Department of Defense customers. These were on top of existing business with allied and Israeli defense entities. These are longer term contracts involving extended delivery, testing, and so on, so revenue recognition is often delayed. In other words, the orders exist, but the revenue is lagging.

Management behaviour reinforces this interpretation. Despite lower reported defense revenue, management actively increased investments in defense R&D and sales. If they were facing structural demand erosion, they would not be increasing spending in R&D. It is true that I do not consider defense a driver of current growth and re-rating - that is all IFC- but defense is a stabilizing influence on earnings with strong competitive hurdles in place. So it is important to clarify its strengths, and assess its reported weakness as temporary.

Peru is a repeatable export model

In 2025, Gilat secured ~$85 million in awards from the Peruvian government. This is a potential source of skepticism around Gilat, since such orders can be considered lumpy, one-off revenue. However, that is not true.

Firstly, within Peru itself, these orders involve multi-year service contracts, maintenance, and operational components. They are not just limited to initial network deployment. Therefore, Peru provides both backlog and revenue visibility over the medium term.

Management wants to make a template out of Peru. The Peru order includes satellite infrastructure, managed services, as well as support. The focus on digital inclusion in that country. More and more emerging market governments are looking for this. Gilat could potentially find new markets in Latin America, Africa, and parts of Asia.

Thus, even though Peru is not a driver of the IFC-related re-rating, it offers potential for durable revenue and service-oriented cash flows over the years. Its visibility also reduces downside risks when other sectors may be between cycles.

Valuation: Incomplete due to transition

GILT does not look cheap on trailing metrics. At 24x adjusted EBITDA, valuation looks stretched, especially if you take current earnings to have peaked. However, that is an assumption that creates the disconnect I have been talking about here.

2025 was a transition year - management has repeatedly said so. So a valuation based on 2025 earnings will fall short - because those figures will be exceptions. ~$450 million was the revenue guidance for 2025. Adjusted EBITDA guidance was $52 million, a 11-12% margin. However, these guidance values do not consider what will happen when the Stellar Blu ramp-up is completed, nor the total shift from retrofit to line-fit. So multiples created out of that guidance are not truly predictive.

So a better way to look at valuation is on a normalized earnings basis, rather than reported adjusted EBITDA. Let’s only assume a modest execution, so we could peg this at $500 million in revenue over the next 12 months as IFC scales, defense revenues are recognized, and Peru continues to contribute. At a 15% EBITDA margin - which is still conservative relative to connectivity businesses with a platform - we get around $75 million in EBITDA.

If we apply a conservative 17x EV/EBITDA multiple in line with mature communications infra peers, we get an enterprise value of ~$1.28 billion. Adjusting for net cash, we get an equity value of ~25 per share.

There are no bullish assumptions here. GILT just needs to complete what it has started, clear backlogs, recognize revenues, complete ramp-up and thereby normalize margins. Current share price growth reflects market confidence in execution, but it still does not reflect a business that has completed its transition.

Risks

The primary risk here is how long it takes to execute IFC cleanly - not demand. Many things could alter the timeline - delays in line-fit certification, slower engine onboarding - which may not negate the opportunity entirely, but still hurt earnings.

Another risk is the manufacturing ramp-up at Stellar Blu. Here, too, many things could hurt the ramping schedule. The business is still transitioning to higher production, so if that gets delayed, margin improvement, too, could be delayed.

We have argued how management looks at Peru as a template. However, government and defense business is lumpy by nature. If the company fails to win in other geographies, these will remain as one-off revenues. They will not add the kind of stability GILT will need to stave off delays elsewhere, and sentiment may wane.

Lastly, valuation itself is a risk. I have argued why there still exists upside despite the sharp run-up. However, much of this will require sentiment to catch up with fundamentals, and even the hint of trouble could force the market to re-rate the stock back to legacy SATCOM multiples.

Conclusion: A Rerating Still in Progress

Gilat’s stock has gone up sharply, but the business still has upside. Gilat is no longer a SATCOM-only business, but its growth is predicated on IFC. However, today’s valuation seems anchored to an earlier profile of GILT.

Given my valuation calculations, and given current stock prices, there is still an upside of ~30% in the next 12-18 months. No aggressive growth assumptions are necessary for that - the company just needs to execute what it has already started. Given that, I would rate this a BUY with modest confidence.

data.title: Pinterest: The 'Year Of Efficiency' Has Arrived, And I'm Loading Up
data.description:
Pinterest (PINS) has struggled in recent months. The most recent slide comes after the company announced plans for a significant layoff. While the advertising landscape may be set to become more competitive, investors appear to be completely ignoring the low valuation of the stock. PINS continues to be profitable on a GAAP basis while maintaining a pristine net cash balance sheet. Even if the company ends up seeing top-line growth decelerate, I expect strong returns on account of the low valuation. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

Pinterest Stock Price

I last covered PINS in November where I rated the stock a strong buy on the low valuation and international momentum. The stock has fallen nearly double-digits since.

It is hard to make an argument that PINS is anywhere as exciting as Meta Platforms (META) or others in the age of generative AI, but I have confidence that the fundamentals will win out in the long term.

PINS Stock Key Metrics

The social media platform has seen considerable weakness as of late in part due to an announcement of plans to reduce around 15% of the workforce. Investors oftentimes view layoffs as a sign of weakness as it might imply that management has less confidence to invest in growth. There indeed was some justification for concern, as the company has seen growth in its lucrative North America monthly active users (‘MAUs’) slow down to less than 1% on a sequential basis.

While the company made up for that slowdown with strong growth in average revenue per user, investors might be wondering how long international strength can offset the domestic slowdown.

It is worth noting that the company is still growing revenues at a robust 17% clip, which is impressive even if it falls short of the stronger results at META.

Where I differ from the market is in my interpretation of the job reduction implications. This may be a “glass half full take,” but I view the layoff as being a direct catalyst towards margin expansion. PINS delivered 200 bps of YoY margin expansion in the third quarter and one analyst has estimated that the layoff might lead to as much as $175 million in annual cost savings. That would be enough to grow adjusted EBITDA by more than 50%.

It bears remembering that PINS is profitable on a GAAP basis and has a balance sheet featuring $2.8 billion of cash versus no debt. It is admittedly hard to get excited about the business, but the financial health of the company is not in question.

PINS is set to report fourth quarter earnings on February 12th after the market close. Consensus estimates call for the company to generate $1.33 billion in revenue, coming in at the high end of guidance of between $1.313 billion and $1.338 billion.

Given the stock’s recent weakness, I wouldn’t be surprised if the company ends up disappointing on revenue, but the market may be overly concerned over the wrong metrics.

Is PINS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

It is possible that the stock has been sliding not only due to the announced layoffs, but due to intensifying competition in social media and online advertising. META continues to increase its dominance through generative AI. OpenAI has released Sora and has begun advertising efforts for ChatGPT. Market sentiment for PINS differs drastically from just several years ago when investors were cheering the company’s successful integration of third party partnerships. Now, the stock trades at just around 12x forward earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain mid-teens top-line growth, with the stock trading at around 3.3x forward sales.

It is possible that consensus estimates prove a tad optimistic, even if META did deliver strong results of their own. I also don’t have any convincing argument to make regarding PINS taking market share in advertising. I mention that because in years past, a common thesis for PINS (and Snapchat (SNAP) as well) was that they may see accelerated growth due to having smaller revenue bases. That notion is seriously under question and I don’t have a clear rebuttal to that.

But the stock still looks attractive here. The low valuation means that expectations - and requirements - of the stock are greatly different than in the past. I see this getting to at least 30% net margins over the long term. GAAP net margins came in at around 9% in the most recent quarter. Between operating leverage and the aforementioned layoff, I see a clear case for accelerated margin expansion in the near term. The stock trades at around 11x long term earnings (based on 30% net margins). That multiple looks too conservative given the net cash balance sheet and already GAAP profitable P&L. Valuations look cheap even if growth rates plunge to the 8% range - I’d challenge readers to find any net cash balance sheet stocks trading at such low multiples with the resilient business model of PINS. Absent multiple expansion, I can see the stock delivering around 12% to 15% returns, driven by both top-line growth and an expanding earnings yield. But I can see the stock re-rating to 4x to 6x sales (equating to 13x to 20x long term earnings). The main catalysts would be the aforementioned margin expansion alongside the already-committed share repurchase program.

PINS Stock Risks

It is possible that PINS is a value trap. Perhaps META or others render the platform obsolete. Perhaps growth rates decelerate even more drastically than expected as the company laps tough comparables. Perhaps generative AI changes the advertising landscape. I am skeptical given the strong balance sheet and potential for operating leverage, but disruption would be the main concern here. On the flip side, while PINS itself might be easily replicable by AI coders, it is instead the network that gives it strength.

PINS Stock Conclusion

PINS is not an exciting AI stock any longer, even if the company looks poised to benefit from both AI-powered recommendations as well as AI-led improvements to the cost structure. The stock has continued to sell off even after the recent layoff announcement, which is ironic given the increased likelihood for margin expansion. PINS is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.
